title: Charmed Life - Chapter 21 (Fragment 1)
text:
Related Worlds

R ELATED W ORLDS

AS ANYONE READING these five e-books knows by now, there are hundreds of different worlds, and some are nicer than others. A new world is formed when there is a big event in History, like a battle or an earthquake, when the outcome can be two or more quite different things. Different things can happen but they cannot exist together, so the world splits into worlds and History goes its separate ways.

Chrestomanci and his team know about other worlds and a number of them have been visited. There are twelve known Related Worlds and each of these worlds is divided into sets, called Series (there are usually nine of these), which are categorised according to the events in History that were the same in them. The numbering of the Related Worlds goes from twelve to one. The numbers go backwards because it’s thought that Series One is the original world of the twelve, and the great Mages of One who first discovered the other worlds did the numbering.

Although each world is different, the worlds may contain some elements that are the same. For example, you might have a number of counterparts in the Related Worlds, but while you and your counterpart may be exact doubles, the chances are your characters will be quite different. If you wanted to find out more about Related Worlds, a book like Other-World Studies, Series III would come in handy.

If you didn’t know about Related Worlds or Series, then you might call them the Anywheres or Almost Anywheres. But if you have studied the Related Worlds you would know how different each Series can be: you may not want to visit Series Eight, a bleak and stony world, or Series Six, where the worlds are covered in ice. And don’t be fooled by Series Eleven, as it’s not a Series at all, but just one world. But you may like Series Five, with its islands and mermaids. And if you’re looking for a good cup of hot chocolate, Series Nine is the place for you.

vocabulary.definition: A fight between armed forces or groups.
vocabulary.example: …a big event in History, like a battle or an earthquake.

vocabulary.definition: Describing a place or situation that is cold, empty, or without hope.
vocabulary.example: Series Eight, a bleak and stony world.

vocabulary.definition: To arrange or classify into groups according to shared characteristics.
vocabulary.example: ...called Series, which are categorised according to the events in History.

vocabulary.definition: The possibilities or likelihoods of something happening.
vocabulary.example: The chances are your characters will be quite different.

vocabulary.definition: People or things that have the same function or position in a different place or situation.
vocabulary.example: You might have a number of counterparts in the Related Worlds.

vocabulary.definition: Being spread over or having a layer on top.
vocabulary.example: Series Six, where the worlds are covered in ice.

vocabulary.definition: Not the same as another or each other.
vocabulary.example: You and your counterpart may be exact doubles, but the chances are your characters will be quite different.

vocabulary.definition: To find something that was not known before.
vocabulary.example: Mages of One who first discovered the other worlds.

vocabulary.definition: A sudden and violent shaking of the ground, often causing damage.
vocabulary.example: …a big event in History, like a battle or an earthquake.

vocabulary.definition: Basic parts or components of something.
vocabulary.example: The worlds may contain some elements that are the same.

vocabulary.definition: Fully correct or precise.
vocabulary.example: Your counterpart may be exact doubles.

vocabulary.definition: To come together or cause to come together into a particular shape or organization.
vocabulary.example: A new world is formed when there is a big event in History.

vocabulary.definition: Useful or convenient to have or use.
vocabulary.example: A book like Other-World Studies, Series III would come in handy.

vocabulary.definition: Frozen water.
vocabulary.example: Series Six, where the worlds are covered in ice.

vocabulary.definition: Lands surrounded by water.
vocabulary.example: Series Five, with its islands and mermaids.

vocabulary.definition: Mythical creatures with the upper body of a woman and the tail of a fish.
vocabulary.example: Series Five, with its islands and mermaids.

vocabulary.definition: More pleasant or agreeable.
vocabulary.example: There are hundreds of different worlds, and some are nicer than others.

vocabulary.definition: Being the first or earliest form of something.
vocabulary.example: Series One is the original world of the twelve.

vocabulary.definition: The result or consequence of an action or event.
vocabulary.example: …when the outcome can be two or more quite different things.

vocabulary.definition: Connected or associated with something.
vocabulary.example: There are twelve known Related Worlds.

vocabulary.definition: A set of related books, events, or things that follow a particular order.
vocabulary.example: Each of these worlds is divided into sets, called Series.

vocabulary.definition: Divides or breaks into parts or groups.
vocabulary.example: The world splits into worlds.

vocabulary.definition: Covered with or full of stones.
vocabulary.example: Series Eight, a bleak and stony world.

vocabulary.definition: To go to a place for a short time.
vocabulary.example: A number of them have been visited.
